hero.eyebrow: Self-Acclaimed Roboticist and Full-stack Engineer (HW and SW), ECE @ Duke
hero.heading: hi, i'm Chloe Gu!
hero.description1: I'm a self-acclaimed roboticist and full-stack engineer (HW and SW) studying Electrical Engineering at Duke. Other than robotics, I'm interested in building little gadgets and tinkering with hardware / code.
hero.description2: My ultimate goal for robots that solve real problems—starting in industrial automation and eventually becoming part of everyday life. Beyond that, I wish they can have human level dexterity and be able to interact with the world like humans do. In research, I'm exploring how robots can learn from humans, model contact-rich interaction, and transfer skills from simulation to the real world.
hero.description3: Some of my hobbies include birding, speedcubing, and photography. Feel free to reach out to me at chloe [dot] gu [at] duke [dot] edu!

projectsIndex.introBlurb: A few of the things I'm most proud of. There's plenty more where these came from.

projectsAll.introBlurb: Everything I've worked on over the years, starting from my sophomore year in high school.

fun.teaserSub: a little about me, plus photography, birding & speedcubing.

about.label: / about me
about.heading: a little about me
about.body: Hihi! I'm Chloe. I was born in Austin, Texas, but I spent most of my life in Shanghai, China before going to college (including staying at the same school for 12 years). Like a lot of people, I love travelling and eating good food. But other than that, I always had a lot of hobbies, and some I still keep today are photography (especially birds!) and solving Rubik's cubes.

photography.title: Photography & Birding
photography.body: Birding has taken me around the world and parks just right beside my home. I have seen around 400+ species of birds to this day, and some of my most memorable species (that I've seen) are Collared Scops Owl and the Japanese Paradise Flycatcher.

speedcubing.title: Speedcubing
speedcubing.body: I have been speed-solving Rubik's cubes since I was in middle school, and I've known how to solve one since 2nd grade. Currently, I can solve a 3x3 cube in around 7 seconds and 15 seconds one-handed. I've also written tutorials, competed internationally, posted videos online, been a cubing coach, and created Rubik's cube mosaics.
